clean_story:
Invisible puppeteers. Hosts turned into tools.

The term parasite comes from Greek parasitos—"one who eats at another’s table." But some don’t just eat. They rewrite the menu.

A parasite doesn’t fight its host. It whispers. The lancet liver fluke, Dicrocoelium dendriticum, invades an ant’s brain, forcing it to clamp onto grass blades at dawn—where a grazing cow swallows it whole. The fluke’s next home. No struggle. Just a hijacked instinct: climb, lock, wait.

The fungus Ophiocordyceps does worse. It sprouts through an ant’s exoskeleton, then pumps chemicals into its muscles, ordering it to die biting a leaf’s vein—the perfect perch for spores to rain on new victims. The ant’s last act isn’t survival. It’s delivery.

Even viruses play this game. Rabies rewires mammals to bite, not flee, turning saliva into a syringe. Toxoplasma in rats erases fear of cats, because cats are its ride to reproduction.

No hypnosis. No magic. Just precise chemical cues, exploiting neural gaps evolution left open. The host’s body stays intact. Its purpose does not.

Three related subjects are zombie ants’ last march, viruses as genetic puppets, and how brains resist invasion.
